ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_03315595); no specific wonton noodle with peanut sesame sauce entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodles, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for wheat-based cooked noodle dish with mixed protein toppings
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for wonton noodle with peanut sesame sauce. A0FJP (cooked noodles) used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for this composite dish found in the clinical terminology hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of wonton noodles with peanut sesame sauce weighs approximately 400 g, comprising noodles, 3–4 wontons, sauce, and any accompanying broth or garnish.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — 麻酱虾肉馄饨面 Noodle With Shrimp & Pork Wantons In Peanut Sesame Sauce
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010060). Carbohydrate is recorded as 0.0 g/100g in the HPB source, which is inconsistent with the known composition of wheat noodles. This is flagged as a data anomaly; the HPB-reported value is preserved as-is per source fidelity policy, with the anomaly documented in notes. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 2.1 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for comparable pork-shrimp wonton noodle dishes (USDA SR Legacy #21376 pork and USDA #15150 shrimp, weighted composite).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wheat Noodles — Analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles (GI ~52, Atkinson et al. 2008)
gi_source.source_match_notes: The HPB source records carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g for this dish, which is a known data anomaly (wheat noodles typically contribute 20–30 g/100g cooked). Because the HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g, the glycemic load per serving calculates to 0.0 g. The GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283). The GI category is 'low' consistent with cooked wheat noodles. GL per serving is recorded as 0.0 reflecting the HPB-reported carbohydrate value; actual GL would be substantially higher if true carbohydrate content (~20–30 g/100g) were used.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; peanut-sesame sauce and soy sauce are primary contributors
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request sauce on the side and limit portion to no more than 150 g to keep sodium intake below 200 mg per eating occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Wheat noodles contribute significant refined carbohydrates (estimated 20–30 g/100g cooked) with minimal dietary fibre, driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation; HPB-recorded carbohydrate of 0.0 g is a data anomaly
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should request wholemeal or lower-GI noodle alternatives, pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glucose spike, and limit portion size to reduce overall glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g) elevates blood pressure risk; pork and peanut-sesame sauce contribute saturated fat and cholesterol (89 mg/100g), compounding cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its high sodium and saturated fat content; choose smaller portions, request reduced-sauce preparation, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 1050 mg/100g exceeds RA threshold; pork contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise consumption; consider substituting pork with shrimp-only or tofu wontons and choosing wholegrain noodles to reduce inflammatory glycaemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Noodle with Shrimp and Pork Wontons in Peanut Sesame Sauce (麻酱虾肉馄饨面) is a Chinese-Singaporean noodle dish featuring springy wheat-based noodles served with plump wontons filled with a mixture of minced shrimp and pork, all tossed in a bold, aromatic peanut-sesame sauce. The sauce is typically made from sesame paste (tahini or Chinese sesame paste), peanut butter, soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and chilli, delivering a rich, nutty, savoury, and slightly spicy flavour profile. The noodles are boiled until al dente and the wontons are either boiled or steamed before being combined with the sauce. Texturally, the dish offers a contrast between the chewy noodles, silky wonton wrappers, and the tender shrimp-pork filling. Clinically, this dish is high in sodium (1050 mg/100g), making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification. The HPB-recorded carbohydrate value of 0.0 g/100g is a known data anomaly; wheat noodles typically contribute 20–30 g/100g cooked, placing this dish in the moderate-to-high glycaemic load category for people with Type 2 Diabetes. Protein content is relatively high at 19.1 g/100g, contributed by shrimp, pork, and wheat gluten. Fat is 10.5 g/100g, primarily from peanut butter, sesame paste, and pork. This dish is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore hawker centres and Chinese restaurants.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010060, confidence 0.95). The carbohydrate anomaly (0.0 g/100g) is documented and preserved per source fidelity policy. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (cooked wheat noodles). All mandatory multilingual names present for Singaporean Chinese dish.
notes: CARBOHYDRATE ANOMALY: The HPB source records carbohydrate_g as 0.0 g/100g and sugar_g as 0.0 g/100g for this dish. This is inconsistent with the known composition of wheat noodles, which typically contribute 20–30 g/100g cooked. The HPB value is preserved as-is per source fidelity policy, but this anomaly is flagged for upstream data correction. Dietary fibre is also recorded as 0.0 g/100g by HPB, consistent with the carbohydrate anomaly. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g is recorded as 0.0 per the HPB source; this is a confirmed HPB-reported value, not a naturally zero nutrient — the dish contains wheat noodles and the 0.0 value is a known data anomaly. sugar_g is 0.0 per HPB source (confirmed zero per HPB, consistent with the carbohydrate anomaly). dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 per HPB source (confirmed zero per HPB, consistent with the carbohydrate anomaly). NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite dish are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010060, and no sufficiently close composite match was found in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. SATURATED FAT: Not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 2.1 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy weighted composite of pork (USDA #10006) and shrimp (USDA #15150) with peanut-sesame sauce components. GI NOTE: GI of 52 is an analogous estimate for cooked wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). Glycemic load per serving is calculated as 0.0 based on HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g; actual GL would be approximately 24–36 per 400 g serving if true carbohydrate content were used. HALAL STATUS: This dish contains pork and is therefore not halal.

Iteration 1: Added GI analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (cooked wheat noodles, Atkinson et al. 2008).

Iteration 1: Documented all -1 sentinel fields in notes per g3 rule.
